session1InCourse1.name: Midterm Peer Review
session1InCourse1.instructions: Please provide thoughtful and constructive feedback for each question below. Your responses will help your peers improve.

session2InCourse1.name: Team Project Milestone 1
session2InCourse1.instructions: Evaluate your team members' contributions to Milestone 1. Be honest but respectful in your assessments.

session3InCourse1.name: Final Peer Evaluation
session3InCourse1.instructions: This is the final evaluation of the semester. Please rank and rate your teammates comprehensively.

session4InCourse1.name: Instructor Feedback
session4InCourse1.instructions: Please share your honest feedback about the instruction quality and course content.

session1InCourse2.name: Sprint 1 Review
session1InCourse2.instructions: Reflect on Sprint 1 and evaluate your teammates. Focus on concrete examples of contribution.

session2InCourse2.name: Sprint 2 Review
session2InCourse2.instructions: Evaluate your team's performance during Sprint 2. Consider code quality, collaboration, and process adherence.

session3InCourse2.name: Final Team Assessment
session3InCourse2.instructions: Provide your final assessment of all team members. This evaluation covers the entire project duration.

session1InCourse3.name: Lab Partner Evaluation
session1InCourse3.instructions: Evaluate your lab partner's problem-solving skills and collaboration during the laboratory sessions.

session2InCourse3.name: Algorithm Design Review
session2InCourse3.instructions: Evaluate your peer's algorithm design quality, correctness analysis, and presentation clarity.

session3InCourse3.name: End-of-Term Feedback
session3InCourse3.instructions: Share your thoughts on the course, your team's work on the final project, and any suggestions for improvement.

questionDetails.questionText: Reflect on your learning progress so far this semester. What concepts have you mastered and what areas need improvement?
How effectively did this team member contribute to group discussions?
Rate your confidence in recursion and higher-order functions. (1 = Not confident, 5 = Very confident)
Rate the contribution of yourself and your team members towards the project.
Evaluate your teammate's performance on the following dimensions.
Which skills did you develop during this milestone? Select all that apply.
Rank your team members by their overall contribution to the project.
Distribute 100 points among the following project aspects based on their importance to your team.
Provide your overall assessment of the class performance this semester.
How would you rate this instructor's teaching effectiveness?
Rank the following course topics by how difficult you found them. (1 = Most difficult)
What suggestions do you have for improving this course?
Summarize your individual contribution to Sprint 1. Include specific tasks you completed.
Rate the contribution of each team member including yourself towards Sprint 1.
Rate the quality of your team's Sprint 1 deliverable. (1 = Poor, 10 = Excellent)
Evaluate your teammate's code quality on the following criteria.
Select all agile practices your team followed well during Sprint 2.
Distribute 100 points among your team members based on their effort in Sprint 2.
Rank your teammates by their technical skill level.
Which area do you think you improved the most in during this course?
Describe your overall learning experience in this course. What will you take away?
Provide constructive feedback on your lab partner's problem-solving approach and collaboration.
Rate your lab partner's collaboration skills. (1 = Poor, 5 = Excellent)
Would you want to work with this lab partner again?
Evaluate your peer's algorithm design on the following criteria.
Rank these data structures by how useful they were in your assignments. (1 = Most useful)
Allocate 100 points to reflect the relative time you spent studying each topic.
Rate each team member's contribution to the final project.
Which aspects of the course were most valuable to your learning? Select up to 3.
Share any final thoughts or suggestions for future iterations of this course.

answer.answer: I have made solid progress with recursion and feel confident about basic tree traversals. Higher-order functions like map and filter are becoming second nature, though I still struggle with environment model diagrams for deeply nested closures.

Understanding higher-order functions has been a major breakthrough. I can now compose functions fluently and debug recursive calls with more confidence. The environment model remains challenging but I am improving steadily with practice.
I feel comfortable with most functional programming concepts covered so far. My main weakness is converting recursive solutions to tail-recursive form and understanding continuation-passing style.
The conceptual foundations are solid, especially around list processing and structural recursion. I want to strengthen my understanding of streams and lazy evaluation before the final exam.
Closures and lexical scoping have become intuitive for me. I still need more practice with the substitution model proofs, but my overall coding ability has grown significantly this semester.
My grasp of recursion is strong and I apply it well in lab assignments. Memoization techniques and dynamic programming concepts are where I need the most additional work.
I have improved significantly in writing modular, reusable code using higher-order functions. The continuation-passing style lab was challenging and I plan to revisit those exercises.
Functional programming was entirely new to me at the start of the semester but I have adapted well. List processing feels natural and I appreciate the elegance of purely functional approaches.
Excellent
Good
Good
Satisfactory
Excellent
Good
Excellent
Good
Outstanding
Very Good
Outstanding
I would appreciate more interactive coding exercises during lectures. The current pace is good but additional worked examples for the harder topics like streams would be helpful.
The lab sessions could benefit from clearer problem statements. Sometimes the expected output format is ambiguous. Otherwise the teaching quality has been excellent.
More practice problems for the environment model would help. Perhaps a weekly optional problem set focused specifically on tracing through closures and scope chains.
I took the lead on designing the class diagram and implementing the core Parser module. I also set up the CI/CD pipeline and reviewed three pull requests from teammates during Sprint 1.

My main contributions were implementing the Storage component and writing integration tests for the save and load functionality. I also helped debug a critical serialization issue that blocked the team for two days.
I focused on the UI layer, building the main window layout and command input handling. I also wrote the user guide section covering basic commands and helped resolve merge conflicts.
I worked primarily on the Model component, implementing the data structures for tasks and events. I also contributed to team discussions on design decisions and attended all standups.
I implemented the undo and redo feature and wrote comprehensive unit tests for the Logic component. I participated actively in code reviews and suggested improvements to our test coverage.

Viktor approaches problems methodically, always starting with a clear analysis before coding. He is patient when explaining his reasoning and open to alternative solutions. One area for improvement would be to consider edge cases earlier in the design phase.

Zara has an excellent intuition for choosing the right data structure. She quickly identifies patterns in problems and proposes efficient solutions. I would suggest she document her thought process more thoroughly for complex algorithms.

Amara is a strong collaborator who actively communicates during pair programming sessions. She catches bugs quickly and has a good eye for optimizing time complexity. She could benefit from exploring more space-efficient approaches.
Ingrid is thorough in her approach and always verifies correctness with multiple test cases. She explains graph traversal concepts very clearly and is always willing to help.
Tomas brings creative solutions to difficult problems. His implementation of the priority queue was particularly elegant. He could improve by adding more inline comments to his code.
Definitely
Probably
Definitely

comment1ToResp1ForQ1InSession1InCourse1.commentText: Excellent self-awareness, Mei Lin. Your identification of closures as a growth area is spot on. I recommend working through the supplementary exercises on environment model diagrams.

comment2ToResp1ForQ1InSession1InCourse1.commentText: Thank you for the feedback, Prof. Khan. I have started the supplementary exercises and they are really helpful for understanding nested closures.

comment1ToResp2ForQ1InSession1InCourse1.commentText: Good progress, Carlos. Consider pairing up with a classmate to practice environment model walkthroughs before the midterm.

comment1ToResp1ForQ2InSession1InCourse1.commentText: Thank you for this evaluation. The peer feedback process is important for team development.

comment1ToResp1ForQ1InSession1InCourse2.commentText: Great summary, Liam. Setting up the CI/CD pipeline early was a strong strategic decision. Keep documenting your design rationale in the developer guide.

comment2ToResp1ForQ1InSession1InCourse2.commentText: Thanks Dr. Vasquez! I will make sure to improve the developer guide documentation for Sprint 2.

comment1ToResp1ForQ1InSession1InCourse3.commentText: Thoughtful feedback, Amara. Encouraging your partner to consider edge cases earlier is exactly the kind of constructive observation we value.

comment1ToResp1ForQ1InSession4InCourse1.commentText: Thank you for the positive feedback. I will continue to incorporate more interactive examples in my teaching.

comment1ToResp1ForQ2InSession2InCourse1.commentText: This rubric evaluation aligns well with my own observations. Carlos has been steadily improving his communication in team settings.

comment1ToResp1ForQ1InSession2InCourse2.commentText: Good evaluation. I notice Anya has been very consistent in following coding standards. Encourage her to also share her documentation practices with the team.

notification1.title: Scheduled Maintenance Window
notification1.message: TEAMMATES will undergo scheduled maintenance on 15 September 2026 from 02:00 to 06:00 SGT. Please save your work and submit any pending evaluations before the maintenance window.

notification2.title: New Feature: Rich Text Responses
notification2.message: You can now use rich text formatting in your feedback responses! Look for the formatting toolbar when answering text questions that support it.
